topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The clinking of champagne flutes and the forced, saccharine laughter of the assembled guests grated on my nerves like sandpaper on glass. I’d managed to navigate the crowded reception with the practiced stiffness of someone trying to hold themselves together while a slow-motion train wreck unfolded just out of sight. Then, there he was. Standing near the buffet, looking annoyingly polished in a suit that probably cost more than my rent, was Mark. My mutual friend, Chloe, had dragged me here under the guise of ‘catching up,’ but all I felt was a tightening in my chest. When our eyes met across the room, the forced smile I was wearing threatened to crack. I immediately pivoted, pretending to be deeply engrossed in a conversation about the questionable quality of the canapés, my jaw tight enough to ache. I just needed a quiet corner, a moment of reprieve from this manufactured happiness.

[story 2]

"Oh, look who decided to grace us with their presence," I muttered under my breath, clutching my drink a little too tightly. The music was too loud, the air thick with perfume and inauthentic joy. And there he was, leaning against a pillar, radiating an aura of effortless charm that always seemed to irritate me. Mark. He looked… fine. Too fine. I could feel the heat rising in my neck, a flush creeping up my cheeks that I desperately tried to mask by focusing intensely on the intricate pattern of the carpet. I deliberately took a long, slow sip of my wine, letting the silence stretch between us just long enough to let the awkwardness settle. When he finally glanced my way, his expression was a carefully constructed mask of pleasant recognition, and I just offered a curt, almost imperceptible nod before turning my attention back to the dance floor, needing the distraction of movement to keep my composure intact.

[story 3]

I took a deep, shaky breath, the scent of expensive lilies suddenly overwhelming. This was precisely the kind of social performance I actively avoided. I watched from across the ballroom as Chloe, bless her oblivious heart, happily engaged in animated conversation with Mark. Every time he turned, our paths seemed magnetically drawn together, and I felt an almost physical urge to retreat into the shadows. My hands were cold, and I consciously kept my posture rigid, a deliberate barrier against any potential interaction. When he finally navigated through the crowd to stand near me, offering a smile that didn't quite reach his eyes, I simply managed a clipped, "Hey," my voice sounding drier than I intended. I didn't wait for any follow-up; I just turned my body slightly away, signaling that the interaction had reached its absolute, unpalatable limit.
